text: The surgeon visualizes the lesion on the sigmoid colon and begins the procedure with a middle to lateral approach. During dissection, the left ureter is identified.
The surgeon dissects the anterior mesenteric artery approximately 1.5 centimeters from the aorta and sections it using a 45-millimeter EndoWrist instrument with a vascular reload.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors and a bipolar fenestrated grasper to dissect the inferior mesenteric vein, which is then clipped and sectioned.

The surgeon employs an extended medial-to-lateral approach for mobilizing the splenic flexure. The inferior mesenteric vein is ligated at the lower border of the pancreas. The superior surface of the pancreas is separated from the retroperitoneum, and the lesser sac is opened. The gastrocolic and pancreatic mesocolic attachments along the tail of the pancreas are divided. A sterile gauze is placed near the splenic hilum before sectioning the gastrocolic ligament.
The surgeon completes the mobilization of the entire left colon by dissecting the total fascia, separating the colon from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon transects the rectum at least five centimeters from the colic cancer using a vessel sealer, preparing the rectal stump for transaction. The rectum is then transected with a 45-millimeter endobriste grain recharge. The colon is extracted through a five-centimeter incision.

The surgeon identifies the proximal transection margin using indocyanine green fluorescence angiography to ensure good vascular supply. The EEA stapler is inserted and secured, performing a colon-hepatic end-to-end anastomosis. The vascular viability of the intestinal tissue is rechecked with indocyanine green fluorescence, confirming a good vascular supply.
